questions.question: Explain how the concept of Virtualization allows the Operating System to efficiently manage the CPU and Main Memory as fundamental resources.
keyPoints: OS abstracts physical hardware into logical entities (virtual CPUs, virtual memory).
CPU virtualization enables multiprogramming via time-sharing.
Memory virtualization (paging/swapping) allows processes to use more memory than physically available.
Provides isolation and protection between processes.

questions.question: Define a Deadlock. State and explain the four Coffman conditions that are necessary for a deadlock to occur.
keyPoints: Deadlock is a state where a set of processes are blocked waiting for resources held by others.
Mutual Exclusion: Only one process can use a resource at a time.
Hold and Wait: Process holds resources while waiting for others.
No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken away.
Circular Wait: A circular chain of processes exists where each waits for a resource held by the next.

questions.question: (a) Compare Shortest Job First (SJF) and Round Robin (RR) CPU scheduling algorithms in terms of throughput, waiting time, and fairness. (b) Explain the Safety Algorithm used in Banker's Deadlock Avoidance technique.
keyPoints: SJF: Optimal for minimizing average waiting time but may cause starvation; Non-preemptive usually.
RR: Good for time-sharing systems, ensures fairness, but higher average waiting time due to context switches.
Safety Algorithm checks if a 'Safe Sequence' of process execution exists.
It compares the 'Need' matrix of all processes with the available 'Work' resources to see if resources can be allocated safely.

questions.question: (a) Explain the 'Critical Section Problem' in the context of resource sharing and state the three requirements a solution must satisfy. (b) Differentiate between Deadlock Prevention and Deadlock Avoidance.
keyPoints: Critical Section: A part of code where shared resources (e.g., printer, data) are accessed.
Requirements: Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting.
Prevention: E.g., Removing 'Hold and Wait' by requesting all resources upfront.
Avoidance: E.g., Banker's Algorithm dynamically checking resource requests to keep system in a safe state.
